overview: The PO Filter processes Gettext PO (Portable Object) and POT (PO Template) resource files. It supports bilingual and monolingual modes, plural forms, domains, and various PO comment types (references, extracted comments, translator comments, context). Encoding is auto-detected from BOM or the `charset` declaration in the PO header entry.

mode.bilingualMode.help:
Controls how `msgid` and `msgstr` entries are interpreted:

- **Bilingual mode** (true): `msgid` is the source text, `msgstr` is the translation. This is the standard PO file convention.
- **Monolingual mode** (false): `msgid` is a real identifier (not source text), and `msgstr` contains the actual text. The `msgid` value becomes the resource name.

In bilingual mode, the fuzzy flag (`#, fuzzy`) maps to the `approved` target property set to "no". Removing or changing this property removes the fuzzy flag from output.
notes: When switching to monolingual mode, the `makeID` option has no effect since `msgid` is used directly as the identifier.

mode.protectApproved.help:
Marks all entries that are **not empty and not fuzzy** with a non-translatable flag, effectively locking approved translations from modification during processing.

Entries with the `#, fuzzy` flag or empty `msgstr` are left unprotected.

mode.wrapContent.help:
Controls whether long content lines in `msgid` and `msgstr` entries are wrapped across multiple quoted strings in the output PO file.

PO files conventionally wrap long lines for readability, with the first line being an empty string and subsequent lines containing the wrapped content.

identification.makeID.help:
Generates resource identifiers from a hash of the `msgid` source text. The generated ID may include:

- A **domain prefix** if the entry belongs to a named domain
- The **`msgctxt` value** as a differentiator ‚Äî two entries with the same source but different context get different IDs

For plural form entries, the ID gets an additional index suffix (e.g., `P3ADE34F0-0`, `P3ADE34F0-1`).

If `msgctxt` contains an Okapi-format context like `okpCtx:tu=123`, the specified ID (123) is used directly instead of generating one.
notes: Generated IDs may not be unique if the source text is not unique within the same domain and has no distinct `msgctxt` values.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when bilingual mode is enabled

identification.includeMsgContextInNote.help: Copies the content of `msgctxt` lines into the resource notes, making context information available alongside extracted comments and references.

output.outputGeneric.help: Produces output in a generic (non-PO) format rather than standard PO format. Useful for converting PO content into a more interoperable representation.

output.allowEmptyOutputTarget.help:
Controls behavior when no translation exists for an entry:

- **Enabled**: writes an empty `msgstr ""` to the output
- **Disabled**: untranslated entries are handled according to the default PO convention (typically copying the source)

inlineCodes.useCodeFinder.help:
Enables inline code detection using the regular expressions defined below. Matched patterns are converted to inline codes (placeholders) rather than translatable text.

The default expression matches:
- **printf-style format specifiers**: `%d`, `%s`, `%2$s`, `%-10.2f`, etc.
- **Escape sequences**: `\n`, `\t`, `\r\n`, `\a`, `\b`, `\f`, `\v`
- **Java-style placeholders**: `{0}`, `{1,date}`, etc.

inlineCodes.rules.help:
Regular expressions that identify inline codes within translatable text. Each rule is applied against extracted content ‚Äî any match becomes a non-translatable inline code.

The default rules cover common PO file patterns (printf specifiers, escape sequences, and curly-brace placeholders).

processingNotes: Encoding is auto-detected from BOM first, then from the `charset` declaration in the PO header (first 1000 chars). If `charset` is literally the string `charset` (template placeholder), the default encoding is used.
If BOM encoding and header `charset` conflict, a warning is generated and the BOM encoding takes precedence.
The output `charset` header declaration is automatically updated to match the output encoding.
Output BOM for UTF-8: only preserved if input was also UTF-8 and had a BOM. Encoding conversion from non-UTF-8 to UTF-8 produces no BOM.
**No language information is updated in the PO header entry.** Language-related fields must be updated manually.
Line-break style (CRLF/LF) in output matches the original input.
Plural forms are processed as a single group with type `x-gettext-plurals`. Forms must appear in sequential order (`msgstr[0]`, `msgstr[1]`, etc.).
Domains are represented as groups with type `x-gettext-domain` and the domain name as the resource name.
Entries without quotes on the first line (e.g., bare `msgid` followed by quoted continuation) are automatically rewritten with an initial empty string.
The following PO flags are currently ignored: `No-Wrap Flag`, `X-Format Flag`, `No-X-Format Flag`.

examples.title: Standard bilingual PO file
examples.description: A typical PO file with header, singular entry, and plural forms. The header contains encoding and plural form configuration.
examples.input:
```po
# PO file for myApp

msgid ""
msgstr ""
"Project-Id-Version: myApp 1.0.0\n"
"Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8\n"
"Plural-Forms: nplurals=2; plural=(n != 1);\n"

msgid "diverging after version %d of %s"
msgstr ""

msgid "You have selected %d file for deletion"
msgid_plural "You have selected %d files for deletion"
msgstr[0] ""
msgstr[1] ""
```

examples.title: Context-based ID generation
examples.description: When `msgctxt` contains an Okapi-format context string (`okpCtx:tu=ID`), the filter uses the specified ID directly instead of generating one from a hash.

examples.title: Domain grouping
examples.description: PO domain directives are extracted as groups with type `x-gettext-domain`. Each domain becomes a separate group in the extracted output.
examples.input:
```po
domain TheDomain1
msgid "Text 1 in domain 'TheDomain1'"
msgstr "Texte 1 dans le domain 'TheDomain1'"
```
